primary_diagnosis.name: Severe plaque psoriasis, acute explosive flare (strep-triggered)

pathophysiology_summary: Plaque psoriasis is a chronic immune-mediated disease of accelerated keratinocyte turnover producing well-demarcated erythematous plaques with dense adherent silvery scale on extensor surfaces/scalp/nails, with Koebnerisation and the Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale is removed). A streptococcal infection and stress have triggered an explosive flare with deep, painful fissures across flexural creases causing mechanical immobility and bleeding. The must-not-miss variants are erythrodermic psoriasis (>90% BSA erythema with impaired thermoregulation, fluid/protein loss and high-output cardiac strain) and generalised pustular psoriasis — both dermatological emergencies — and secondary staphylococcal infection/sepsis through fissured skin; here erythroderma is excluded (<90% BSA, stable vitals, no thermoregulatory failure) and there is no fever/pustulation/pus. Discriminators: thick silvery scale with Auspitz sign and extensor distribution distinguish it from greasy-yellow-scaled seborrhoeic dermatitis, and the absence of monomorphic punched-out vesicles/erosions and fever argues against eczema herpeticum. Management is intensive emollients (to soften plaques and stop fissuring), a potent topical corticosteroid, analgesia, and urgent dermatology referral for systemic therapy (topicals alone are inadequate for this severity), with an emergency safety-net for erythroderma/pustular flare/sepsis.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic silvery-scaled extensor plaques with Auspitz sign on a 10-year background, strep-triggered.
Screen for >90% BSA erythema, thermoregulatory failure and widespread pustulation — a dermatological emergency.
Fissured skin can superinfect — screen for pus/spreading redness/fever.
Greasy yellow scale, facial/scalp — not thick silvery extensor plaques with Auspitz sign.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise a severe plaque flare, screen for erythroderma/pustular variants and sepsis, and manage with emollients + potent topical + urgent dermatology referral.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic plaques; not erythrodermic, no pustulation/sepsis.
<90% BSA, stable vitals, no thermoregulatory failure or pustulation — remains an emergency safety-net.
Working diagnosis: intensive emollients + potent topical steroid + analgesia + urgent dermatology referral; erythroderma/pustular/sepsis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Erythrodermic (>90% BSA) or generalised pustular psoriasis with systemic upset/thermoregulatory failure
red_flags.clinical_significance: A dermatological emergency (fluid/heat/protein loss, high-output failure, infection risk) needing admission.

red_flags.description: Secondary staphylococcal infection/sepsis through fissured skin (pus, spreading hot redness, fever/rigors)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fissured barrier can superinfect and become systemic.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening for >90% BSA erythema, pustulation or thermoregulatory failure that would mandate emergency admission.
Managing a severe flare with topical steroid alone without emollients or urgent dermatology referral for systemic therapy.
Not screening fissured skin for superinfection.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Intensive emollients + potent topical steroid + analgesia and an urgent dermatology referral; in-person review within days; ED for erythroderma, pustular flare or sepsis.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing an evolving erythrodermic/pustular flare or secondary sepsis, or under-treating with topicals alone.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Severe psoriasis: potent topical corticosteroids + emollients for symptom control; refer for systemic therapy (methotrexate/biologics) or phototherapy; treat streptococcal triggers; recognise erythrodermic/pustular psoriasis as emergencies.
